perturbation.original_text:
Receiving Party shall:
(i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for two (2) years following the date that Disclosing Party first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and 2(c) of this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text:
Receiving Party shall:
(i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for one (1) year following the date that Disclosing Party first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and 2(c) of this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-disclosure period to one year may conflict with trade secret laws that protect confidential business information for longer periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act provides legal recourse for misappropriation of trade secrets. While the Act does not explicitly define a minimum protection period, a one-year period may be deemed insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, particularly in technology-intensive industries, potentially undermining the protection afforded by the Act if the trade secret remains valuable for a longer duration.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-disclosure period from two years to one year, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws. The Law Explanation states that a one-year period may be insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, potentially undermining the protection afforded by the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act if the trade secret remains valuable for a longer duration. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' as information that '(a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use'. If a trade secret retains its economic value for longer than one year, limiting the protection to one year directly contradicts the intent to protect that trade secret as defined in the law.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees and consultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party within 30 days of discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees and consultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification period to 30 days after discovery of unauthorized use or disclosure may violate data breach notification laws that require prompt notification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington State Data Breach Notification Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Washington's data breach notification law requires notification to affected individuals 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay,'. A 30-day delay could be deemed an unreasonable delay, thereby conflicting with the state law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.255.010: Personal information—Notice of security breaches. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.255 > Section 19.255.010 19.255.005 << 19.255.010 >> 19.255.020 PDF RCW 19.255.010 Personal information — Notice of security breaches. (1) Any person or business that conducts business in this state and that owns or licenses data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system to any resident of this state whose personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the personal information was not secured. Notice is not required if the breach of the security of the system is not reasonably likely to subject consumers to a risk of harm. The breach of secured personal information must be disclosed if the information acquired and accessed is not secured during a security breach or if the confidential process, encryption key, or other means to decipher the secured information was acquired by an unauthorized person. (2) Any person or business that maintains or possesses data that may include personal information that the person or business does not own or license shall notify the owner or licensee of the information of any breach of the security of the data immediately following discovery, if the personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person. (3) The notification required by this section may be delayed if the data owner or licensee contacts a law enforcement agency after discovery of a breach of the security of the system and a law enforcement agency determines that the notification will impede a criminal investigation. The notification required by this section shall be made after the law enforcement agency determines that it will not compromise the investigation. (4) For purposes of this section and except under subsection (5) of this section and RCW 19.255.030 , notice may be provided by one of the following methods: (a) Written noti
perturbation.justification: The change from "immediately" to "within 30 days" directly contradicts the intent of Washington's data breach notification law, which requires notification 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay,'. RCW 19.255.010 requires immediate notification to the owner or licensee of the information of any breach of security of the data immediately following discovery. The introduction of a 30-day window allows for a delay that could be deemed unreasonable, thus creating a legal contradiction. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition applies because the changed text sets a deadline (30 days) that potentially violates state law requiring expedient notification.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Washington, and the parties further consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts sitting in King County, Washington, unless no federal subject matter jurisdiction exists, in which case the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the Superior of King County, Washington. Company waives all defenses of lack of personal jurisdiction and forum non conveniens.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Washington, and the parties further consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts sitting in Snohomish County, Washington, unless no federal subject matter jurisdiction exists, in which case the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the Superior of Snohomish County, Washington. Company waives all defenses of lack of personal jurisdiction and forum non conveniens.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying Snohomish County instead of King County for venue may conflict with mandatory venue provisions in some specific types of lawsuits under Washington law, especially if the cause of action arose in King County.
perturbation.law_explanation: Washington Civil Rule 82.01 dictates the proper county for venue in civil actions. Changing the venue to Snohomish County when the cause of action arises or the defendant resides in King County violates this rule, potentially making the venue clause unenforceable. For instance, if the primary place of business relevant to the contract is in King County, a lawsuit filed in Snohomish County could be challenged under this rule.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Revised Code of Washington § 4.12.025 (2022) - Action to be brought where defendant resides—Optional venue of actions upon unlawful issuance of check or draft—Residence of corporations—Optional venue of actions against corporations. :: 2022 Revised Code of Washington :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Rev. Code of Wash. 2023 Rev. Code of Wash. 2022 Rev. Code of Wash. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Revised Code of Washington Title 4 - Civil Procedure Chapter 4.12 - Venue—Jurisdiction. 4.12.025 - Action to be brought where defendant resides—Optional venue of actions upon unlawful issuance of check or draft—Residence of corporations—Optional venue of actions against corporations. Universal Citation: WA Rev Code § 4.12.025 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next RCW 4.12.025 Action to be brought where defendant resides—Optional venue of actions upon unlawful issuance of check or draft—Residence of corporations—Optional venue of actions against corporations. (1) An action may be brought in any county in which the defendant resides, or, if there be more than one defendant, where some one of the defendants resides at the time of the commencement of the action. For the purpose of this section, the residence of a corporation defendant shall be deemed to be in any county where the corporation: (a) Transacts business; (b) has an office for the transaction of business; (c) transacted business at the time the cause of action arose; or (d) where any person resides upon whom process may be served upon the corporation. (2) An action upon the unlawful issuance of a check or draft may be brought in any county in which the defendant resides or may be brought in any division of the judicial district in which the check was issued or presented as payment. (3) The venue of any action brought against a corporation, at the option of the plaintiff, shall be: (a) In the county where the tort was committed; (b) in the county where the work was performed for said corporation; (c) in the county where the agreement entered into with the corporation was made; or (d) in the county where the corporation has its residence. [ 1998 c 56 § 1; 1985 c 68 § 2; 1983 c 31 § 1; 1965 c 53 § 168; 1927 c 173 § 1; RRS § 205-1. Prior: 1909 c 42 § 1; Code 1881 § 49; 1877 p 11 § 50; 1869 p 13 § 50; 1860 p 101 § 488; 1854 p 220 § 494.] Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the venue from King County to Snohomish County. The Law Explanation indicates this change could contradict Washington Civil Rule 82.01 if the cause of action arises or the defendant resides in King County. Scraped Law Snippet 2 (RCW 4.12.025) states an action may be brought in any county where the defendant resides or transacts business. If the defendant resides or transacts business in King County, changing the venue to Snohomish County could be a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation supports this as the change 'may conflict with mandatory venue provisions...especially if the cause of action arose in King County.'

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party. All sections of this Agreement relating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other party. All sections of this Agreement relating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 30 days may be deemed unreasonable or insufficient, particularly if it impairs a party's ability to protect its trade secrets after termination, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Washington Law)
perturbation.law_citation: E.g., Badgett v. Security State Bank, 116 Wn.2d 563, 569, 807 P.2d 356 (1991)
perturbation.law_explanation: Washington law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. While a 30-day notice period for termination is not inherently illegal, a court might find it unreasonable if it significantly impairs a party's ability to protect its confidential information or otherwise undermines the benefits the party reasonably expected from the agreement, thus breaching the implied duty. The Badgett v. Security State Bank case helps illustrate the principles behind implied duty of good faith in Washington contracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute of limitations | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of limitations A statute of limitations is any law that bars claims after a certain period of time passes after an injury. The period of time varies depending on the jurisdiction and the type of claim. There are statutes of limitations for both civil and criminal causes of action . They may begin to run from the date of the injury, the date it was discovered, or the date on which it would have been discovered with reasonable efforts. Many statutes of limitations are actual legislative statutes , while others may come from judicial common law . Compare: s tatute of repose . [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS courts statutory interpretation wex definitions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a 90-day notice for termination, while the changed contract reduces this to 30 days. The Law Explanation suggests that under Washington law, a court may deem a 30-day notice unreasonable if it impairs a party's ability to protect confidential information, breaching the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition, where time-sensitive obligations (the notice period) do not align with legal requirements (the implied duty of good faith). While the provided law snippet discusses statutes of limitations, which is not directly relevant, the Law Explanation provides a scenario where the reduced timeframe can create a legal issue.
